front: Work: in this novel, Arkady Kirsanov brings his nihilist friend home to his father's estate Marino
Work: Bazarov duels with the foppish Pavel (shooting him in the leg) after kissing the servant Fenichka
Work: a young doctor dies after cutting himself on his own scalpel while treating a typhus patient and failing to cauterize the wound
Work: the servant Fenichka bears a child for her master Nikolai
Work: a father trains his kilt-wearing son based on Rousseau's physical theories and Voltaire's mental indoctrination, after failing to receive an inheritance from his rich aunt
Work: a son dismantles a romantic rival's arguments praising British manufacturing, French art, and German philosophy while trashing his home country
Work: this short story collection about the lives of serfs contributed to the abolition of serfdom and got its author arrested
Work: 'Hamlet of the Shchigrovsky District' — a man at a party tells the narrator to call him Hamlet; Vasily Vasilyevich sticks his tongue out at his reflection
Work: 'Singers' — the Gabbler and Blinkard become emotional as the Turk wins a singing contest; narrator later hears a boy calling for 'Antropka'
Work: 'Khor and Kalinych' — the first story in the collection; the landowner Polutikin controls two contrasting peasants
Work: 'Bezhin Lea' — Pavlusha drives away wolves but later dies falling off a horse
Work: a man named Radilov only began to weep for his wife's death when he saw a fly land on her eye
Work: 'Kasyan from the Beautiful Lands' — a dwarf who spends his days catching nightingales
Work: 'A Living Relic' — a beautiful serf named Lukerya is immobilized by disease but enjoys nature and teaches an orphan girl to sing
Work: Nekrasov published this collection in The Contemporary, aiding the abolition of serfdom
Work: the teenager Vladimir falls for Princess Zinaida, then discovers his own father is her secret lover
Work: autobiographical novella fictionalizing Princess Catherine Shakhovskoy as Zinaida
Work: the protagonist is shocked to find his father striking Zinaida with a riding crop
Work: framed by Vladimir Petrovich and two other gentlemen recounting their earliest romantic memories
Work: popularized the term 'superfluous man' through the diary of the dying aristocrat Tchulkaturin
back: Diary of a Superfluous Man (Ivan Turgenev)

front: Work: the protagonist confronts a Prince during a dancing game of La Confidente, leading to a duel
back: Diary of a Superfluous Man (Ivan Turgenev)

front: Work: essay contrasts Hamlet's egocentrism with Don Quixote's self-sacrifice, calling them 'poles of the human axis'
Work: the confectioner's daughter Gemma Roselli enthralls the Russian Sanin in Frankfurt
Work: Hemingway's novel mocking Sherwood Anderson takes its title from this Turgenev novella
Work: a play about the handsome tutor Aleksei Belyaev and the unhappily married Natalya Petrovna
back: A Month in the Country (Ivan Turgenev)

front: Work: Stanislavski first deployed his acting system for a revival of this play, starring Olga Knipper
back: A Month in the Country (Ivan Turgenev)

front: Work: set in Baden-Baden; Grigory Litvinov finds heliotropes on his windowsill
Work: satirizes Alexander Herzen via the liberal character Gubaryov among intellectuals at Baden-Baden
Work: focused on Lavretsky; the unfaithful urban wife Varvara is contrasted with the title provincial group
back: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk (Ivan Turgenev)
work: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk

front: Work: Goncharov accused Turgenev of plagiarizing The Precipice for this novel
back: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk (Ivan Turgenev)
work: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk

front: Work: the widow Marya tries to impress the poet Panshin with her daughter Liza's piano playing; Liza sometimes titles translations
back: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk (Ivan Turgenev)
work: Home of the Gentry / A Nest of Gentlefolk

front: Work: 'Mumu' — a deaf-mute porter drowns his dog in the same river where he rescued her
Work: 'The Dog' — a ghostly apparition inspires the narrator to buy 'Little Tresor,' who saves him from a rabid animal
Work: 'A King Lear of the Steppes' — the muscular Harlov is mistreated by his daughters, in a story inspired by Shakespeare
Author: lived in Paris with the mezzo-soprano Pauline Viardot; wrote three libretti for her; menage a trois
Author: caricatured by Dostoevsky as the out-of-touch liberal poet Semyon Karmazinov in Demons; longstanding feud
Author: Isaiah Berlin wrote about this author's 'complacent ambivalence' in the essay 'Turgenev and the Liberal Predicament'
Author: Dostoevsky mocked this author as needing a telescope to see Russia because he lived in Paris
Author: jailed for writing an obituary of Gogol
